PARAGRAPH 1
To solve this problem efficiently we must write a function that parses date strings and filters them based on century logic. The core challenge involves converting a year string into an integer and then determining which century it belongs to. A century is defined as a period of one hundred years. The first century covers years 1 through 100. The second century covers years 101 through 200. Generally the nth century includes years from 100 times n minus 99 up to 100 times n. For example the nineteenth century spans from 1801 to 1900 inclusive. This means if we have a year like 1899 it falls within the nineteenth century. However a year like 1900 is the final year of the nineteenth century while 1901 starts the twentieth century. Our algorithm must extract the year portion from each date string provided in the input list. The input format is strictly YYYY-MM-DD so we can split the string by hyphens or slice the first four characters to get the year. Once we have the year as an integer we calculate the expected century for that year. The formula to find the century of a given year is simply floor of year minus 1 divided by 100 plus 1. Alternatively we can check if the year falls within the specific range defined by the target century. The range starts at 100 times the target century minus 99 and ends at 100 times the target century. If the extracted year is greater than or equal to the start year and less than or equal to the end year then we keep that date string. We add it to our result list. This approach avoids complex date parsing libraries and relies on simple string manipulation and integer arithmetic which is very fast for large datasets. We must ensure our code handles edge cases like years ending in zero or years at the start of a century correctly. The function will iterate through the list once making it linear in time complexity which is optimal for this task. We will define the function name as filter_dates_by_century to make the code readable. The function accepts two parameters named date_list and target_century. Inside the function we initialize an empty list called result. We loop through each item in date_list. For each item we extract the year substring. We convert this substring to an integer.
